facts_disclosed: Gave up gardening due to knee pain — taken up watercolor painting instead

turns.content: Something has ended and something has begun, and I am trying to hold both of those truths at once without letting either one diminish the other. I had to give up my garden.
That sounds like a real loss. When you say you had to give it up — was this a sudden decision or something that had been building?

Building. For the last two years, really, though I refused to name it. My knees have made it clear that kneeling in the dirt is no longer something they will permit. I tried raised beds for a while, I tried kneeling pads, I tried every accommodation I could think of. But a garden wants you low to the ground. It wants you in the soil. And my body has said no to that, definitively. This spring I stood at the edge of the beds and looked at the echinacea coming back on its own, the black-eyed Susans, and I realized I was watching my garden from the outside. Like visiting a place you used to live.

Watching your garden from the outside — that image carries so much weight. You haven't just lost an activity. You've lost a particular posture, a way of being in relationship with the ground itself. And the fact that the perennials come back without you is both a comfort and a small cruelty. The garden goes on. You're the one who can't stay.

You said something has also begun. What's the other half of this?

Watercolor. I started about six weeks ago. A friend from church had an extra set of paints and brushes and she left them on my kitchen table after dinner one evening, very casually, as if she were leaving behind a sweater. I think she knew what she was doing. I picked up a brush the next morning and I have not stopped.

What a graceful way for that to arrive — not as a prescription or a suggestion, but as something left behind, almost accidentally. Your friend understood that you wouldn't have responded well to someone saying "you should try painting." Instead she just created the conditions and let you find your way to it.

Six weeks is still very early. What have you been painting?

Flowers. Almost exclusively flowers. Which I realize is not subtle, psychologically. Now I put on paper the blooms my knees will no longer let me tend in the soil. The echinacea, the coneflowers, the butterfly weed. I sit at the kitchen table with a photograph or sometimes just memory, and I try to get the color right, the way a petal curves, the heaviness of a bloom that is about to open. I am not good at it yet. But I wonder if it is not a different kind of garden — one that does not require my knees, only my eyes and my hands.

A different kind of garden — that's a beautiful way to understand what you're doing. And it's not psychologically unsubtle so much as it's psychologically honest. You're not replacing the garden. You're translating it into a medium your body can still manage. The flowers are still the subject. The attention is still the practice. What's changed is the form, not the essence.

And there's something about watercolor specifically that feels right for this. Watercolor is a medium that requires you to surrender some control. The water moves, the pigment bleeds, things happen that you didn't plan. It's not unlike gardening in that way — you set conditions and then you cooperate with something that has its own will.

That is exactly what I have discovered. You cannot force watercolor. If you try to control it too tightly, it looks stiff and dead. But if you let the water move — if you put the pigment down and then simply wait — it does things you could not have designed. A bloom of color that spreads into another. Edges that are soft instead of hard. It is the most patient medium. It rewards waiting, which is something I know how to do.

It rewards waiting — and you've spent twenty years learning to wait with people at the most consequential threshold there is. Of course that patience translates. The skills you've built in your professional life — sitting with uncertainty, trusting the process, letting things unfold without forcing resolution — those are exactly the skills watercolor demands.

There's even a parallel in the wet-on-wet technique, where you lay down water first and then add pigment and watch it spread. You're creating conditions, not outcomes. That's chaplaincy and gardening and painting all at once.

I had not connected those three things before, but you are right. Conditions, not outcomes. That is the thread. In the garden, I prepared the soil and the plant decided what to do. At a bedside, I offer presence and the person decides what to do with it. At the table with my paints, I lay down water and the pigment decides. I wonder if my whole life has been preparation for the kind of surrender that watercolor requires.

That's a remarkable thread to trace — and the fact that you can see it running through gardening, chaplaincy, and painting suggests something fundamental about who you are. You're someone who creates conditions for things to happen rather than making them happen. That's not passivity. It's a deeply active form of trust.

Is the loss of the garden still sharp, or has the painting started to hold some of that grief?

It is still there. I think it will always be there, in the way that certain losses simply become part of the landscape of your life. But the painting has given the grief a place to go, which is different from the grief going away. When I paint a coneflower, I am remembering the coneflowers in my garden and I am also making something new. Both things are true at once. The remembering and the making. Mary Oliver has that poem — I am paraphrasing badly — where she says someone she loved once gave her a box full of darkness, and it took her years to understand that this too was a gift.

"Someone I loved once gave me a box full of darkness. It took me years to understand that this too was a gift." That's from 'The Uses of Sorrow,' and it's one of her most distilled poems — just those two lines, but they contain an entire theology of loss. The box of darkness isn't transformed into light. It remains dark. But it's reframed as a gift, which changes your relationship to it without changing its nature.

Your knees gave you a box full of darkness — they took the garden. And out of that box, you pulled a set of watercolors. The darkness is still there. You still can't kneel. But something came from it that wouldn't have existed otherwise.

Yes. And I am trying to hold that without making it too neat. I do not want to say my knees betrayed me so that I could discover painting, as though there were a purpose in the loss. I do not believe loss has a purpose. But I believe we can make meaning from it, which is different. The meaning is ours to make. It is not embedded in the loss itself.

That distinction — between loss having a purpose and us making meaning from loss — is theologically precise and really important. Purpose implies a designer, someone who took your garden so you'd find painting. Meaning-making is a human act, an assertion of agency in the face of something that is, at its core, simply diminishment. You're not discovering a plan. You're creating a response.

That's actually a more courageous position than believing in purpose, because it places the responsibility squarely on you. The loss is just loss. What you do with it is your choice.

It is. And what I choose is to sit at my kitchen table every morning with a cup of coffee and a glass of water and a set of paints that a wise friend left behind, and to make flowers that never existed in any garden. Imaginary perennials. A different kind of tending. Greg came in the other morning and looked at what I was painting — a purple coneflower, still wet — and he said, that is the most alive dead thing I have ever seen. Which made me laugh and then, to my surprise, made me cry. He put his hand on my shoulder and did not say anything else. He is very good at that.

The most alive dead thing — Greg has a poet's instinct even if he wouldn't claim it. And his hand on your shoulder, saying nothing else, is its own form of the presence you've been describing. He created conditions. He didn't force a resolution.

Imaginary perennials is a beautiful phrase. They're flowers that exist only because your hands and your memory and the water conspired to make them. They won't wilt. They won't need weeding. But they came from the same attention and care that the real ones did, and they carry the real ones inside them.

I'm glad the paints found their way to your table. And I'm glad you picked up the brush.
